bindings.title: Detroit Rooted sign
bindings.excerpt: The main sign anchors the page in plant records, local evidence, and the idea that a front yard can behave like a civic ecology board.
bindings.source_summary: Michigan Flora turns species occurrence, habitat notes, and specimen evidence into a usable local palette for choosing what belongs in a Southeast Michigan frontage.

bindings.title: Rain Garden Swale board
bindings.excerpt: The big explainer board ties the world to a buildable Great Lakes rain-garden logic instead of vague green aesthetics.
bindings.source_summary: The Wild Ones Grand Rapids design lays out runoff retention, bioswale thinking, shoreline protection, and practical plant-community zoning in a Michigan-adjacent context.
bindings.source_image_alt: Screenshot of the Wild Ones Grand Rapids native garden design page

bindings.title: Native seedling trays
bindings.excerpt: The tray cluster maps to the limited-palette strategy: know a small set of plants well and repeat them with intention.
bindings.source_summary: Homegrown National Park frames native planting as a practical composition problem built from palette limits, layered habitat, and gradual yard conversion.
bindings.source_image_alt: Screenshot of the Homegrown National Park native landscape design page

bindings.title: Yard plan blueprint
bindings.excerpt: The central map turns the front yard into an actual site plan with zones, heat pockets, downspouts, and runoff paths.
bindings.source_summary: This design page is useful as a real precedent for translating plant communities, runoff, and phasing into a legible household plan.
bindings.source_image_alt: Screenshot of the Wild Ones Grand Rapids native garden design page

bindings.title: Soil sample tray
bindings.excerpt: The segmented tray stands in for the quieter layer beneath the flowers: substrate, moisture, and the record of where plants actually thrive.
bindings.source_summary: Michigan Flora is less a mood board than a record system, grounding plant choice in specimens, maps, and habitat conditions rather than wishlist gardening.

bindings.title: Shrub drift zone stone
bindings.excerpt: The marker stone maps to the straight-species question: what gets planted here is not just visual but ecological and genetic.
bindings.source_summary: Wild Ones makes the case for prioritizing straight-species natives over designer cultivars when the goal is biodiversity, resilience, and habitat value.
bindings.source_image_alt: Screenshot of the Wild Ones nativars and cultivars page

bindings.title: Gardening tools set
bindings.excerpt: The tool cluster points to the social side of the work: chapters, volunteers, and people actually making these yards together.
bindings.source_title: Grand Rapids Discussion with Designers Amy Heilman & Rebecca Marquardt
bindings.source_summary: A Wild Ones designer discussion that grounds the page in community practice, chapter culture, and real native-garden implementation talk.
bindings.source_image_alt: YouTube thumbnail for the Wild Ones Grand Rapids designer discussion

bindings.title: Front-yard ecology checklist
bindings.excerpt: The clipboard is the part of the scene that says this is not fantasy landscaping: you can actually phase the bed, call 811, and start with one section.
bindings.source_summary: This guide breaks native-yard work into palette, vision, and stepwise bed planning, which is exactly why the checklist belongs in the scene.
bindings.source_image_alt: Screenshot of the Homegrown National Park native landscape design page
